perturbation.original_text: Governing Law; Venue. The validity, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delawar e, excluding its conflict of law principles. The parties agree that all litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be brought in the state courts of the State of Minnesota and the United States District Courts located therein, and the parties hereby submit to the exclusive personal jurisdiction and venue of such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Governing Law; Venue. The validity, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delawar e, excluding its conflict of law principles. The parties agree that all litigation or other legal proceedings arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be brought in the state courts of the State of Minnesota, and the parties hereby submit to the exclusive personal jurisdiction and venue of such courts. The parties agree that resolution will be sought with arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting a section which mandates arbitration, it makes the original venue rules uncertain. This contradictory section could cause confusion about whether disputes are to be resolved through litigation in Minnesota courts or through arbitration, undermining the clarity and enforceability of the venue clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act favors arbitration agreements. However, by creating ambiguity about whether arbitration or litigation is the proper venue for disputes, the modified text could lead to challenges in enforcing either the arbitration clause or the venue selection clause, potentially resulting in increased litigation costs and delays. 9 U.S.C. § 2 requires the agreement to arbitrate be in writing, and the contradictory language creates doubt about its enforceability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a structural flaw by adding an arbitration clause that conflicts with the original venue clause specifying Minnesota courts. This addition creates uncertainty about the dispute resolution process. Snippet 2, referencing 9 U.S. Code § 2, affirms the validity and enforceability of arbitration agreements. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the ambiguity created by this conflicting language, suggesting it could undermine the enforceability of either clause, which supports that a legal contradiction was created.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. T he Recipient acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its promises or agreements contained herein will result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including specific performance and injunctive relief, in the event of any breach or threatened breach or intended breach of t his Agreement by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient understands and agrees that the Disclosing Party is providing the Confidential Informat ion to the Recipient in reliance upon this Agreement, and the Recipient will be fully responsible to the Disclosing Party for any damage s or harm caused to the Disclosing Party by a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or any of its Representatives. The Recipient acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its promises or agreements contained herein will result in irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including specific performance and injunctive relief, in the event of any breach or threatened breach or intended breach of t his Agreement by the Recipient. However, the Recipient is not responsible for any breaches.
perturbation.explanation: By adding an explicit contradiction at the end ('However, the Recipient is not responsible for any breaches'), the entire remedies section is undermined. It directly contradicts the initial statement about the Recipient being responsible for damages resulting from a breach, making it harder for the Disclosing Party to seek legal recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the fundamental principles of contract law concerning remedies for breach of contract. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 outlines the purpose of remedies, which is to protect the injured party's expectation interest. By adding a contradictory statement that the Recipient is not responsible for breaches, the clause effectively nullifies the intended remedy and undermines the entire purpose of the contract, potentially making the remedies section unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a clear legal contradiction by explicitly stating the recipient is 'not responsible for any breaches,' while the original text holds them 'fully responsible' for damages resulting from a breach. This undermines the contract's enforceability by negating the remedies available to the disclosing party in case of a breach, thus failing to protect the injured party's expectation interest. The LLM's law explanation, while not supported by a snippet, accurately describes the general principles of contract law regarding remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire understanding and agreement of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all other oral or written representations and understandings. This Agreement may be amended or modified only in writing signed by a n authorized representative of each party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire understanding and agreement of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all other oral or written representations and understandings. This Agreement may be amended or modified only in writing signed by a n authorized representative of each party. This agreement can be modified without written consent.
perturbation.explanation: The inclusion of 'This agreement can be modified without written consent' directly contradicts the prior sentence which explicitly requires written modification. This contradiction creates uncertainty about how the agreement can be modified, potentially leading to disputes and making it difficult to enforce the original terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-209 addresses the modification, rescission and waiver of contracts for the sale of goods. While this agreement may not strictly fall under UCC 2-209, the principle remains that a contract's stated modification procedure should be clear and consistent. Adding a contradictory statement about modification without written consent creates ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the modification clause, and creates uncertainty around how the contract can be modified.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a direct contradiction regarding contract modification. The original text states amendments require a signed writing. The added sentence 'This agreement can be modified without written consent' directly opposes this requirement. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight how this undermines the enforceability of the modification clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-209 (2), supports the principle that a signed agreement excluding modification except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified. While the agreement may not strictly fall under UCC 2-209, the principle still applies, and the added sentence creates a clear legal contradiction.
